document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide
root.summary: Front matter for the Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide. It introduces the Food Assistance eligibility rules and is aimed at business analysts, not technical administration. The guide covers the full determination flow: non-financial requirements (citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency), assistance unit and household composition rules, alien sponsor deeming and battered-alien exceptions, resource and income calculations, benefit computation, and sanction assessment. It also includes sanctions for controlled substance use, intentional program violations, work non-compliance, voluntary quit, child support enforcement, drug-related felony, fleeing felon, parole violation, fraud, firearms sales, and trafficking. Pages 45–46 contain legal notices, privacy policy, and trademarks only.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Preliminary note for the document, directing readers to review the Notices section on page 45 before using the information or the supported product. Includes Merative copyright information for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide, applying to versions 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2. It introduces the guide’s purpose and scope, then outlines eligibility foundations for Food Assistance: non-financial requirements (citizenship, alien status, SSN, residency), assistance unit and household composition rules, and key household categories such as homeless persons, institutional residents, exceptions, and optional members. Useful as the program overview and eligibility-rule entry point.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Cúram Food Assistance eligibility rules covering household composition and disqualification. Defines boarders, live-in attendants, roomers, students and student exemptions, elderly/disabled household exceptions, and separate assistance units. Explains who counts as household members, ineligible members, and disqualified members (e.g., IPV, fraud, drug trafficking, fleeing felon). Covers ABAWD status, work registration and work requirement exemptions, non-participation and good cause rules, voluntary quit penalties, striker rules/exceptions, and invalid resource transfer household ineligibility.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Cúram Food Assistance rules on pages 25–34 cover invalid resource transfers and penalties, sponsored alien deeming and exceptions, head-of-household selection, expedited assistance eligibility, categorical eligibility, and alien sponsor definitions/exemptions. It also details resource and income calculation steps, including deemed and pro-rated income/resources, gross and net income limits, exemptions for elderly/disabled/categorically eligible households, and deductions used in eligibility testing. Useful for rules on household composition, financial deeming, expedited benefits, and benefit eligibility calculations.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Food Assistance eligibility and benefit rules covering deductions, utility allowances, net income, proration, and sanctions. Explains earned income, standard, medical, dependent care, child support, and shelter deductions; shelter utility options (SUA/LUA/telephone) and pro-rating rules; net countable income limit; benefit calculation and initial-month proration. Also summarizes sanction assessment and penalties for controlled substance use, IPV, work non-compliance, voluntary quit, child support enforcement, drug-related felony, fleeing felony, parole violation, fraudulent information, firearm sale, and trafficking.

Front matter and program guide for Cúram Income Support Food Assistance (8.1, 8.1.1, 8.1.2). Introduces the Food Assistance rules and outlines the full eligibility flow: non-financial requirements (citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency), assistance unit and household composition, alien sponsor deeming, resource and income calculations, benefit computation, and sanction assessment. Also describes initial month proration and major sanctions for controlled substance use, IPV, work non-compliance, voluntary quit, child support, drug/fleeing felon, parole, fraud, firearms, and trafficking.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section provides legal notices: sample data/disclaimer, copyright license terms for sample programs, the Merative privacy policy URL, and a trademarks list covering Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX, and others. It also states the document’s © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark and copyright notices for Merative and third-party brands (IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, UNIX), plus a privacy policy link and sample-code licensing terms. Notes that sample programs are provided “AS IS” without warranty and may be copied, modified, and distributed under the Merative Client Agreement. Useful as legal/branding reference rather than technical content.

Legal and licensing notices for the publication: personal and enterprise reproduction is allowed only with proprietary notices preserved, but redistribution or derivative works outside the stated limits require Merative consent. It disclaims warranties, export violations, and patent licenses, and says non-Merative product info is unverified. Also notes external websites are not endorsed and includes © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024. Useful if you need usage rights, disclaimers, or legal/trademark references rather than technical content.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: This contents section lists the structure of the Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide. It includes the guide’s main topics: program overview, non-financial requirements (citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency), assistance unit and household determination, alien sponsor rules, food assistance calculations, and sanction assessment. It also lists front/back matter such as notes, edition, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful for navigating to specific policy areas in the guide.

children.title: 1 Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program Guide
1.1 Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program overview
Food Assistance overview
children.summary: Overview of the Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program guide. Explains that Food Assistance serves low- and no-income households through cards/coupons for purchasing food, and eligibility is determined from captured evidence using business rules. Key topics include non-financial rules (citizenship, SSN, residency), assistance unit determination (household composition, head of household, inclusion/exclusion), and program rules. Intended as a reference for business analysts; no technical implementation details are included.

Overview of the Cúram Income Support Food Assistance Program guide. It explains that Food Assistance determines household eligibility and entitlement using captured evidence evaluated against business rules, for low- and no-income households receiving food cards/coupons. The section is a business analyst reference, not technical documentation. Key topics are non-financial rules (citizenship, SSN, residency), assistance unit determination (household composition, head of household, inclusion/exclusion), and program rules that can make a household ineligible.

children.title: 1.2 Non-financial requirements
Citizenship and alienage
Citizenship
children.summary: Explains non-financial Food Assistance eligibility rules focused on citizenship and alien status. A person qualifies through one of 10 citizenship conditions, including U.S. citizen, U.S. non-citizen national, certain Native American and Hmong/Laotian-related categories, trafficking victims, or eligible alien. Also introduces eligible alien and qualified alien definitions and notes that qualified aliens must meet additional criteria. Useful for checking whether a household member meets citizenship/alienage requirements before further eligibility review.

children.title: Eligible alien
children.summary: Defines who qualifies as an eligible alien for Food Assistance. An eligible alien must be both a qualified alien and meet the qualified alien eligibility criteria for Food Assistance. This section sits within non-financial requirements and follows general citizenship rules. Useful for determining alienage-based eligibility and whether a household member can satisfy non-financial eligibility requirements.

children.title: Qualified alien
children.summary: Defines “qualified alien” for Food Assistance eligibility and places it within the non-financial citizenship/alienage rules. A person must meet one of nine specified conditions to be categorized as a qualified alien, and qualified aliens must also satisfy additional eligibility criteria to receive benefits. This section is the gateway for determining whether a non-citizen can qualify before applying other program rules.

children.title: Qualified alien eligibility criteria
children.summary: Lists the Food Assistance “qualified alien” eligibility rules. It explains who counts as a qualified alien and who may qualify based on status or circumstances, including lawful permanent residents, refugees, asylees, parolees, withheld deportation, Cuban/Haitian entrants, Amerasian immigrants, and battered aliens. It also gives age-, disability-, residency-, veteran-, military-, and spouse-based pathways, including the 22-condition framework and examples of qualifying conditions.

Explains Food Assistance non-financial eligibility rules for citizenship and alienage. Covers 10 citizenship paths, including U.S. citizen, U.S. non-citizen national, certain Native American/Hmong-Laotian categories, trafficking victims, and eligible aliens. Defines “eligible alien” as both a qualified alien and meeting program-specific eligibility criteria. Defines “qualified alien” with nine status categories, including lawful permanent resident, refugee, asylee, parolee, withheld deportation, Cuban/Haitian entrant, Amerasian immigrant, and certain battered aliens. Also introduces 22 qualified-alien eligibility pathways.

children.title: Social Security Number (SSN)
Newborn exception
children.summary: Explains the SSN requirement for newborns: households may enroll a newborn without an SSN immediately, but must provide the SSN or proof of application by the next certification period or within six months, whichever is later. Includes context on general SSN rules, residency, and concurrent out-of-state benefits, but the main point is the newborn SSN exception.

Explains Social Security Number requirements for Food Assistance: each individual must have an SSN or apply for one and provide it once assigned. Includes a newborn exception allowing enrollment without an SSN initially, but the SSN or proof of application must be provided by the next certification period or within six months, whichever is later. Also briefly covers related residency rules and concurrent out-of-state benefit restrictions.

children.title: Residency
children.summary: Residency rules for Food Assistance: the household must live in the state where it applies, all household members must be alive, and an individual cannot receive benefits if deceased. It also states that a person receiving concurrent benefits in another state is not eligible to receive benefits there. The section follows SSN and newborn SSN exception requirements and precedes assistance unit determination.

Defines Food Assistance non-financial eligibility requirements: citizenship/alienage, SSN, and residency. Lists 10 citizenship paths (including U.S. citizen, non-citizen national, certain Native American/Hmong-Laotian categories, trafficking victims, and eligible aliens). Explains “eligible alien” as a qualified alien who also meets program criteria. Defines nine qualified-alien categories and introduces 22 eligibility pathways. Also covers SSN rules, newborn SSN exception, and state residency/concurrent benefit restrictions.

children.title: 1.3 Assistance unit determination overview
Household composition
Eligible household members
Homelessness
children.summary: Explains how Food Assistance assistance units are formed around the designated head of household, including household composition, household determination, and related eligibility rules. Defines eligible household member groupings, including individuals living alone, separately purchasing/preparing meals, groups eating together, homeless persons, and residents of exempt institutions. The homelessness subsection begins the criteria for being considered homeless, including lacking a fixed nighttime address or using shelters or temporary institutions as a primary nighttime residence.

children.title: Institution
children.summary: Defines Food Stamp/Food Assistance eligibility rules for people in institutions and household composition. Institutional residents are generally ineligible unless they qualify under six exemptions: subsidized elderly housing, substance abuse treatment facilities, disabled/blind group living arrangements, battered women/children shelters, or nonprofit homeless shelters. Also explains household member exceptions: certain people living together are treated as a single assistance unit (spouses, children under 18 under parental control, and persons under 22 with parents), and optional members such as boarders, foster care individuals, and live-in attendants may be included.

Explains how Food Assistance households are formed around a designated head of household, using composition, determination, and related eligibility rules. Defines eligible household member types: a person living alone, people separately buying/preparing meals, groups buying/preparing together, homeless individuals/groups, and residents of exempt institutions. Begins the homelessness criteria, including lacking a fixed regular nighttime address or using a shelter or temporary institution as a primary nighttime residence.

children.title: Household member exceptions
children.summary: Defines when people living together must be treated as the same Food Assistance unit. Spouses living together, children under 18 under another household member’s parental control, and people under 22 living with natural/adoptive/step parents must be included together. Also notes that some members can be optionally included, such as boarders, certain foster care individuals, and live-in attendants who buy and prepare food with the household, with their income/resources counted.

children.title: Optional household members
Boarder
children.summary: Defines who counts as a boarder: a person or group living with others (not in a commercial boarding house) who pays reasonable compensation for lodging and meals. Explains that paying less than reasonable board does not make someone a boarder, and the household member providing board is counted with spouse/children. Sets reasonable compensation thresholds based on meal frequency. Also covers elderly/disabled household composition exceptions, including when an elderly/disabled person and spouse may form a separate assistance unit or must be included with others.

Explains optional household members for Food Assistance. Households may choose to include certain people in the assistance unit, and if included, their income/resources count and they are counted in household size. This does not apply when someone is required to be included under household member exceptions (spouses, certain children, or persons under 22 with parents). Examples of optional members include boarders, foster care individuals placed by a government foster care program, and live-in attendants who buy and prepare meals with the household.

Explains how Food Assistance assistance units are formed around a head of household using mandatory and optional household member rules. Covers eligible unit types: single person, separate meal buyers, groups buying together, homeless individuals/groups, and residents of exempt institutions. Defines homelessness criteria and notes institutional residents are generally ineligible unless exempt. Lists mandatory household-member exceptions: spouses living together, certain children under 18, and people under 22 living with parents. Also describes optional members such as boarders, foster care individuals, and live-in attendants, whose income/resources count if included.

children.title: Household composition special circumstances
Elderly/disabled living with others
Disabled and Elderly Exception
children.summary: Explains the Disabled and Elderly Exception for Food Assistance: other household members’ gross income must not exceed 165% of the poverty level, excluding the elderly/disabled individual and spouse. Defines disability using 13 qualifying conditions, including SSI, Social Security disability/blindness, VA disability or aid-and-attendance status, Railroad Retirement disability, interim assistance, Medicaid disability coverage, and state disability benefits. Also notes that live-in attendants and roomers may be separate Food Assistance units, with their income/resources counted and household size included.

children.title: Disability
children.summary: Defines the disability criteria and related food assistance rules. It lists 13 ways an individual can be considered disabled, including SSI, Social Security disability/blindness, VA disability, railroad retirement disability, interim assistance, Medicaid disability-related assistance, and state general assistance. It also notes the Disabled and Elderly Exception income test: other household members’ gross income must not exceed 165% of poverty. Finally, it explains when live-in attendants and roomers may be treated as a separate Food Assistance unit and how their income/resources are counted.

Explains when an elderly/disabled person living with others may form a separate Food Assistance unit. An individual age 60+ with a spouse, if any, and a permanent disability may be separate if the Disabled and Elderly Exception is met; otherwise they must be included with the household they buy and prepare food with. The section also covers boarder rules and reasonable compensation for lodging/meals.

children.title: Separate Food Assistance unit
children.summary: Explains when a live-in attendant or roomer can be treated as a separate Food Assistance unit. Eligible separate units include live-in attendants who do not buy and prepare meals with the household, and roomers furnished lodging for compensation but not meals, unless household member exception rules require inclusion. Their income and resources are counted, and they are included in the separate unit size. Also notes the disabled and elderly exception income test and lists 13 disability criteria used to determine disability status.

Covers special household composition rules for Food Assistance. Explains when elderly/disabled people living with others may form a separate unit if the Disabled and Elderly Exception is met (other household income must be 165% or less of poverty), and when spouse must be included. Defines disability via 13 qualifying criteria. Also covers boarders and reasonable compensation for lodging/meals, and when live-in attendants or roomers can be separate Food Assistance units, including that their income/resources count in the separate unit.

children.title: Household determination
Non-household members
Student
children.summary: Defines the student eligibility rule for Food Assistance. An individual enrolled at least half-time in higher education is generally ineligible unless they meet a student exemption. Exemptions include being 17 or younger or 50 or older, physically or mentally unfit, receiving Cash Assistance under Title IV, caring for a dependent under 6, caring for a dependent age 6–12 when child care is unavailable for work/class requirements, or being a full-time single parent caring for a child age 6–11.

Defines household-determination exclusions for Food Assistance. Lists non-household members who live in the home but are not counted in eligibility, income/resources, or household size: people who do not share food prep/purchase, live-in attendants, roomers, commercial boarding house residents, ineligible students, nonexempt institution residents, spouses of addicts/alcoholics in treatment facilities, and individuals already eligible on another Food Assistance delivery (except certain shelter residents). Also states the student rule: half-time higher-education students are generally ineligible unless they meet one of several exemptions.

children.title: Ineligible household members
Able Bodied Adults Without Dependents (ABAWDs)
children.summary: Defines Able Bodied Adults Without Dependents (ABAWDs) for Food Assistance and the rules tied to their three-month time limit. Covers eligibility criteria (age 18–49, no children under 18 in household, fit for work, not pregnant, not receiving SSI, not exempt), countable months across states, and how partial months are treated. Explains work requirements (80 hours/month), regain of eligibility after 80 hours in 30 days, a one-time three-month extension, exemption reasons (transportation, homelessness, health), and system configuration for ABAWD tracking.

Defines ineligible household members for Food Assistance: people in the home who fail SSN, citizenship/alienage, residency, or child support cooperation rules, plus ABAWDs who exceed the time limit. Also explains ABAWD rules: age 18–49, no children under 18 in household, fit to work, not pregnant, not on SSI, and not otherwise exempt. Covers the 3-month limit in 36 months, counting months across states, partial months not counting, work requirements, exemptions, regain eligibility, and system configuration.

children.title: Disqualified household members
Work requirements
Employment offer rules
children.summary: Lists work non-participation excuses and rules for refusing job offers. Notes that a member must accept a bona fide offer of suitable employment unless good cause exists. Good cause for refusal includes jobs that fail to materialize, offer fewer than 30 hours/week, or pay below federal minimum wage thresholds. Suitability good cause covers wages below applicable minimums, piece-rate jobs with low hourly yield, union-related conditions, strike/lockout sites, unreasonable health/safety risk, physical or mental unfitness, and early jobs outside the person’s major field of experience.

This section explains Food Assistance work rules. Household members must register for work or be registered, unless exempt or covered by a valid non-participation reason. It lists work-eligible exemptions (for example, children, older adults, disabled persons, caregivers, students, certain workers, and others) and non-participation reasons such as illness, lack of childcare, transportation problems, domestic violence, or emergency. It also states that members must accept bona fide suitable job offers unless good cause exists, with detailed refusal and suitability exceptions based on pay, hours, labor disputes, safety, fitness, and field of experience.

children.title: Voluntary quit
children.summary: Defines when a person is considered to have voluntarily quit or reduced work effort for Food Assistance eligibility. General rule: quitting or cutting hours without good cause is disqualifying; an exception notes a government employee fired for striking is treated as having voluntarily quit. It also defines reduced work effort as working under 30 hours per week after a voluntary reduction without good cause. Lists many good-cause reasons, including quitting before application, low hours/earnings, exemptions, comparable new job, employer demand, self-employment termination, discrimination, unreasonable work conditions, school enrollment, relocation, or employer-initiated hour cuts.

Explains which household members are disqualified from Food Assistance and how sanctions affect eligibility, income counting, and household size. Lists major disqualifying sanctions/violations (IPV, trafficking, fraud, controlled-substance sales, firearms, probation/parole, fleeing felon, drug felony, work-registration failure, voluntary quit). Also covers work requirements: registration, exemptions, non-participation reasons, employment offer/suitability rules, and voluntary quit/reduced work effort good-cause exceptions.

children.title: Household disqualification
Striker
children.summary: Explains Food Assistance ineligibility rules related to strikers. Households with a striker are generally ineligible unless the person qualifies for a striker exemption or striker exception (eligible the day before the strike). Defines exemptions for certain employees affected by striking, lockouts, non-bargaining-unit workers fearing injury, and workers already exempt from work requirements. Also notes whole-household disqualification if striker rules or invalid resource transfer rules are failed, and that these rules apply at application and reassessment.

children.title: Invalid resource transfer
children.summary: Explains Food Assistance penalties for invalid resource transfers. If a household member transfers countable resources improperly within 3 months before application or during certification, the entire household is ineligible once total resources exceed the limit. It defines the calculation steps, exceptions for certain same-household transfers, and the ineligibility period table: 1 month up to $249.99 over the limit, 3 months for $250–$999.99, 6 months for $1000–$2999.99, 9 months for $3000–$4999.99, and 12 months for $5000+; applies to application and reassessment.

Explains household-level Food Assistance disqualification. A household is ineligible if any member fails striker rules or invalid resource transfer rules, and these rules apply at application and reassessment. Covers striker ineligibility, exemptions (e.g., lockout, non-bargaining-unit workers fearing injury, certain work-requirement exemptions) and the striker exception (eligible the day before the strike). Also details invalid resource transfer rules, exceptions, calculation steps, and the ineligibility periods tied to excess resource value: 1, 3, 6, 9, or 12 months.

children.title: Deemed members of a financial unit: sponsored alien
children.summary: Explains when a sponsored alien can be deemed a member of the financial unit: the household member must be an alien, and the sponsor’s income/resources (and spouse’s if living with the sponsor) are deemed to an alien in the assistance unit. Also covers head of household selection for Food Assistance, including assignment and replacement, and introduces expedited Food Assistance, which issues the first month within 7 days for certain low-income, high-expense, or migrant/seasonal farm worker households.

Covers Food Assistance household determination. Defines non-household members (roomers, attendants, ineligible students, certain institution residents, etc.) and student exemptions. Describes ineligible household members for SSN, citizenship/alienage, residency, child support sanctions, and ABAWD time limits, including 3-month/36-month rules, exemptions, work requirements, regain eligibility, and waivers. Explains disqualified members/sanctions and work rules (registration, exemptions, non-participation, employment refusal, voluntary quit). Also covers household-level disqualification for striker status and invalid resource transfers with 1–12 month ineligibility periods.

children.title: Head of household determination
children.summary: This section explains how Food Assistance requires a designated head of household, how the caseworker records and may change that person, and which assistance unit the head is assigned to. It also notes that the head may be eligible, excluded/non-household, or optionally excluded. The section briefly covers expedited Food Assistance: first-month benefits issued within 7 days for households with little income, high housing/utility costs, or migrant/seasonal farm workers between jobs, subject to full eligibility rules.

children.title: Expedited Food Assistance
Expedited Food Assistance households
Destitute household
children.summary: Defines when a household qualifies as a destitute household for expedited Food Assistance. A household is destitute if its only income for the application month came before application from a terminated source, if the only income is from a new source and no more than $25 is expected by the 10th day after application, or if it has income from both a terminated and new source. Also notes expedited food assistance criteria and related categorical eligibility rules.

This section defines expedited Food Assistance eligibility. Households qualify if they have gross monthly income under $150 and liquid resources of $100 or less, are destitute migrant/seasonal farm worker households with resources of $100 or less, or have combined gross income and resources below rent/mortgage plus utilities. It also defines “destitute household” as income from a terminated source, only a new source with no more than $25 expected by day 10, or both terminated and new income. It briefly covers categorical eligibility rules and exemptions.

Explains expedited Food Assistance for households needing immediate first-month benefits, issued within 7 days instead of 30. Eligible situations include very low income, housing/utility costs exceeding income, or a migrant/seasonal farm worker between jobs. Notes expedited rules apply only to the first month and the household must still meet all Food Assistance eligibility rules to continue receiving benefits. Also describes using application summary evidence and eligibility checks to determine expedited potential.

children.title: Categorically eligible Food Assistance
Categorical eligibility
children.summary: This section explains food assistance categorical eligibility and expedited eligibility. It lists the three expedited food assistance tests, defines a destitute household, and states that households receiving certain other benefits are automatically financially eligible. For categorically eligible food stamp households, six requirements do not apply: resource, gross income, net income, residency, sponsored alien, and SSN rules. Use this node for eligibility exceptions and special qualification pathways.

children.title: Categorically eligible household definition
children.summary: Defines when a household is categorically eligible for Food Assistance: all members must receive Cash Assistance, Diversion payments, SSI/State Supplementary Payments, or General Assistance. It also lists exceptions that negate categorical eligibility (institutionalized household, full disqualification, IPV, drug-related felony, striker rules). Notes how non-household, ineligible, and disqualified members are treated, with SSN/residency exceptions for some ineligible members. Ends with the definition of an alien sponsor and sponsor deeming basics.

children.title: Exceptions to categorically eligible households
children.summary: Explains when a household that is otherwise categorically eligible for Food Assistance loses that status: if the entire household is institutionalized in a non-exempt facility, fully disqualified, disqualified for IPV, disqualified for a drug-related felony, or ineligible under striker rules. Also notes who is excluded from the categorically eligible household (non-household members, ineligible members, disqualified members), with SSN and residency exceptions waived for ineligible members under categorical eligibility. Briefly introduces alien sponsor definition and deeming rules.

children.title: Non-household members, ineligible household members, disqualified household members
children.summary: Explains who is counted in a categorically eligible Food Assistance household and when categorical eligibility is lost. Non-household members, ineligible household members, and disqualified members are generally excluded; ineligible members failing SSN or residency rules are still included because those rules are waived. Also lists exceptions that make a household not categorically eligible (institutionalization, full disqualification, IPV, drug felony, striker rules) and begins the definition of alien sponsor and deeming rules.

Explains categorical eligibility and expedited Food Assistance. Covers the three expedited eligibility tests and the definition of a destitute household. States that recipients of certain other benefits are automatically financially eligible, so resource and income tests do not apply. For categorically eligible households, six rules are waived: resource, gross income, net income, residency, sponsored alien, and SSN requirements. Useful for eligibility exceptions and special qualification pathways.

Defines how Food Assistance assistance units are formed around a designated head of household, including mandatory and optional members, eligible unit types, homelessness and institution rules, boarders/foster/live-in attendant handling, and elderly/disabled separate-unit rules with the 165% poverty test. Also covers household determination exclusions, sponsor/deeming, SSN/residency requirements, voluntary quit and striker sanctions, invalid resource transfers, expedited first-month benefits within 7 days, and categorical eligibility exemptions from resource/income/residency/sponsored alien/SSN rules.

children.title: 1.4 Alien sponsor
Sponsorship deeming exceptions
children.summary: Lists sponsorship deeming exceptions for alien applicants. Specifies groups not subject to sponsor deeming, including refugees, parolees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, certain LPRs, indigent aliens (12 months), naturalized citizens, aliens with 40 qualifying quarters, cases where the sponsor dies or the alien leaves the U.S., Affidavit of Support cases, battered aliens (12-month exemption), and trafficking victims. Also outlines battered-alien rules for the first 12 months and notes continued exemption conditions after 12 months.

children.title: Battered alien: first 12 months
children.summary: Explains sponsor deeming exceptions and battered-alien rules. Lists categories exempt from sponsor deeming, including refugees, parolees, asylees, certain LPRs, indigent aliens for 12 months, naturalized citizens, 40-quarter workers, deceased sponsors, permanent departures, affidavits of support, battered aliens, and trafficking victims. The “battered alien: first 12 months” subsection says income is not deemed for 12 months if the alien is battered by a U.S. citizen/LPR spouse or parent, is a refugee, meets household/connection criteria, and does not live with the abuser. The next subsection begins post-12-month conditions.

children.title: Battered alien: after 12 months
children.summary: This section explains sponsor deeming exceptions for alien households and the special battered-alien rule. It lists aliens exempt from sponsor deeming, including refugees, asylees, certain LPRs, indigent aliens, trafficking victims, and battered aliens/related family members. It also states that battered aliens may be exempt from income deeming for the first 12 months if specific battery, refugee, household, and connection-to-benefits criteria are met. The “after 12 months” part begins the conditions for continued sponsor deeming exemption.

Defines alien sponsor and sponsor deeming rules for Food Assistance. A sponsor is someone who signed an Affidavit of Support and may have income deemed to the alien, unless an exception applies. Lists sponsor deeming exceptions: refugees, parolees, asylees, certain Cuban/Haitian entrants and LPRs, indigent aliens for 12 months, naturalized citizens, 40-quarter workers, deceased sponsors, permanent departures, Affidavit of Support cases, battered aliens/family, and trafficking victims. Also introduces battered-alien deeming rules for the first 12 months and continued exemption after 12 months.

children.title: 1.5 Food Assistance calculations
Resource calculations
Resource deeming
Sponsor to alien deeming
Sponsorship deeming exceptions
children.summary: Lists exceptions to sponsor deeming rules for alien applicants. Covers categories not subject to deeming, including refugees, parolees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, indigent aliens for 12 months, organizational sponsors, naturalized citizens, aliens with 40 qualifying quarters, deceased or departed sponsors, certain battered aliens, and trafficking victims. Also defines the battered-alien first-12-month exemption and notes sponsor resource deeming: count sponsor/spouse resources, subtract $1,500, then deem the remainder unless an exception applies.

Explains sponsor-to-alien resource deeming for Food Assistance. A sponsor’s countable resources (and spouse’s if living in the home) are totaled, $1,500 is subtracted, and the remainder is deemed to the alien unless an exception applies. Defines who counts as a sponsor and notes an alien may have multiple sponsors. Also lists sponsor deeming exceptions, including refugees, parolees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, 12-month indigent alien exemption, organizational sponsors, naturalized citizens, 40 qualifying quarters, deceased/departed sponsors, certain battered aliens, and trafficking victims.

Defines Food Assistance resource deeming. Household countable resources include deemed resources and must stay under $2,000, or $3,000 if a member is age 60+ or disabled. If an individual is a sponsor, sponsor-to-alien deeming applies: count sponsor’s resources (and spouse’s if living in the home), subtract $1,500, and deem the remainder to the alien. Also defines sponsor criteria and notes multiple sponsors are possible. Lists deeming exceptions, including refugee, parolee, asylee, Cuban/Haitian entrant, indigent alien, organizational sponsor, naturalized citizen, 40 quarters, deceased/departed sponsor, certain battered aliens, and trafficking victims.

children.title: Total countable resources
children.summary: Explains how to calculate total countable resources for each individual and household by adding each person’s countable resources plus any deemed resources. Also introduces income calculation rules for Food Assistance, including gross income ≤130% FPL and net income ≤100% FPL unless exempt, and begins sponsor income deeming steps. Includes a continuation of battered alien sponsor-deeming exemptions after 12 months if abuse is recognized by a judge/ALJ and still linked to need for benefits.

Explains Food Assistance resource calculations and deeming. Household countable resources, including deemed resources, must stay under $2,000, or $3,000 if a member is age 60+ or disabled. Shows sponsor-to-alien deeming: count sponsor (and spouse if in home) resources, subtract $1,500, and deem the remainder to the alien. Defines sponsor criteria and notes multiple sponsors. Lists major deeming exceptions: refugee, parolee, asylee, Cuban/Haitian entrant, indigent alien, organizational sponsor, naturalized citizen, 40 quarters, deceased/departed sponsor, certain battered aliens, and trafficking victims.

children.title: Income calculations
Income deeming
children.summary: Explains sponsor income deeming for Food Assistance. For each sponsor in the financial unit, total countable gross unearned income from all sources and countable gross earned income, including self-employment, with spouse income included if the spouse lives in the home. This section sits within broader income/resource calculation rules and follows prior discussion of battered alien exemptions from sponsor deeming and total countable resource steps.

children.title: Pro-rated income gathering
children.summary: Explains how to prorate income for each ineligible household member whose income is shared. For each such person, calculate countable gross unearned income, divide it equally among all household members, and count everyone’s share except the ineligible member’s share. Repeat the same process for gross earned income, including self-employment income. This section sits alongside sponsor-to-alien deeming and precedes the gross countable income steps.

children.title: Gross countable income
children.summary: Introduces the start of the gross countable income calculation for each individual in the financial unit whose income is counted. It signals a 12-step process and follows earlier rules for sponsor-to-alien deeming and pro-rated income gathering. Useful if you need the procedure for determining countable income, including earned and unearned income treatment, before applying Food Assistance eligibility rules.

children.title: Net income calculations
Income deductions
children.summary: Explains how household net income is computed for food assistance and which deductions/disregards apply. Covers the order of deductions: 20% earned income deduction, standard deduction, excess medical, dependent care, child support, and shelter expenses. Details eligible medical, dependent care, child support, homeless shelter, excess shelter, and utility allowances (SUA, LUA, telephone allowance), including elderly/disabled exceptions and pro-rating rules for ineligible members’ expenses. Useful for understanding income-based eligibility and shelter/utility deduction calculations.

children.title: Net countable income
children.summary: Explains how net countable income is calculated as adjusted income after all deductions, and sets the net income limit at or below 100% of the Federal Poverty Limit unless exempt. Also covers shelter expense treatment when using SUA/LUA/telephone allowances or sharing costs with non-household members, including pro-rating rules and the utility standard exception. Begins the Food Assistance benefit calculation steps: maximum allotment by household size, 30% of net income, subtracting to get the monthly allotment, and a minimum $16 allotment for certain 1–2 person households.

Explains food assistance net income calculations. Covers the deduction sequence: 20% earned income deduction, standard deduction, excess medical, dependent care, child support, and shelter expenses. Details allowable medical, dependent care, child support, homeless shelter, excess shelter, and utility deductions, including SUA/LUA/telephone allowance rules, elderly/disabled exceptions, and pro-rating for ineligible members’ expenses. Also defines allowable utility costs and how utility standards are selected. Useful for calculating monthly net countable income and shelter deductions.

Explains Food Assistance income calculations. Covers gross and net income tests against 130% and 100% FPL, sponsor deeming, pro-rating income of ineligible members, and a 12-step gross income aggregation process. Also details net income deductions in order: 20% earned income deduction, standard deduction, excess medical, dependent care, child support, and shelter expenses. Includes homeless and excess shelter rules, elderly/disabled exception, utility allowance selection (SUA/LUA/telephone), and pro-rated expense handling.

children.title: Benefit calculation
Initial month proration
children.summary: Explains how to calculate Food Assistance benefits for the initial month of eligibility based on the application day, following the general benefit calculation rules. The section appears after net countable income and standard benefit allotment steps, and begins the four-step initial-month proration process. It also sits among related shelter expense rules, including treatment of prorated shelter costs, shared expenses with non-household members, and the rule that utility standards (SUA/LUA/telephone allowance) are not prorated.

Explains Food Assistance benefit calculation after net countable income is determined. The standard method has three steps: find the maximum allotment by household size, multiply net monthly income by 30%, and subtract that amount to get the net monthly allotment. It also notes a $16 minimum allotment for eligible one- or two-person households when the result is above zero but below $16. The section then begins the four-step process for initial-month proration, which is based on the application day.

Covers Food Assistance eligibility calculations for resources, income, deductions, and benefits. Resources must stay under $2,000, or $3,000 if a member is age 60+ or disabled, with sponsor-to-alien deeming and exceptions. Income rules test gross income at 130% FPL and net income at 100% FPL, including sponsor deeming, prorated ineligible-member income, and standard deductions for earned income, medical, dependent care, child support, and shelter. Benefit amount uses the allotment chart minus 30% of net income, with a $16 minimum in some small households, plus initial-month proration.

children.title: 1.6 Sanction assessment overview
Sanction assessment
Using Food Assistance in the sale of controlled substance
children.summary: Explains Food Assistance sanctions for specific violations. Covers the rule that a person must not have been found guilty of using Food Assistance in the sale of controlled substances, with penalties of 2 years for a first sanction and permanent ineligibility for a repeat sanction. Also summarizes Intentional Program Violation (IPV) penalties and work requirement noncompliance penalties, including escalating ineligibility periods based on prior sanctions.

children.title: Intentional Program Violation (IPV)
children.summary: This section explains Food Assistance sanctions for Intentional Program Violation (IPV). A household member must not be an IPV; penalties depend on prior IPV sanctions: 12 months ineligible if no prior sanction, 24 months if a previous Level 1 IPV exists, and permanent ineligibility if a previous Level 2 or higher IPV exists. It appears alongside other sanction rules, including controlled substance sales and work requirement non-compliance.

children.title: Work requirements
children.summary: This section explains Food Assistance work requirements and sanctions for noncompliance. A household member must meet work provisions unless exempt or has a valid non-participation reason. It also lists escalating penalties for work non-compliance: 1 month for no prior sanction, 3 months for a prior Level 1 sanction, and 6 months for a prior Level 2 or higher sanction. It briefly references related work rules and notes prior sanction level affects penalties.

children.title: Voluntary quit
children.summary: Explains voluntary quit rules for Food Assistance: an individual must not voluntarily leave a job or reduce work effort without good cause. It lists sanction levels and penalties: 1 month for no prior sanction, 3 months after a prior Level 1 sanction, and 6 months after a prior Level 2+ sanction. The page also briefly references related concepts for child support enforcement, drug-related felony, and fleeing felon sanctions, each resulting in Food Assistance ineligibility.

children.title: Child support enforcement
children.summary: Requires Food Assistance applicants to assign all support rights, including child and spousal support, and to cooperate with CSED unless good cause exists. Good cause includes risk of physical or emotional harm, pending adoption proceedings, assistance from a social agency regarding adoption, or conception by incest or rape. A current child support enforcement sanction makes the individual ineligible for Food Stamp benefits. Also listed nearby are sanctions for voluntary quit, drug-related felony, and fleeing felon status.

children.title: Drug-related felony
children.summary: This section states that an individual must not have been convicted of a felony involving illegal drugs committed after 23 August 1996. If a current drug-related felony sanction exists, the penalty is ineligibility for Food Assistance benefits.

children.title: Fleeing felony
children.summary: This section states Food Assistance eligibility rules for fleeing felons: an individual must not be a fleeing felon, and if a current fleeing felon sanction exists, the individual is ineligible for Food Assistance benefits. It appears alongside related sanction topics on voluntary quit, child support enforcement, and drug-related felony, each with similar ineligibility penalties.

children.title: Parole violation
children.summary: This section lists disqualifying sanctions for Food Assistance. It states that an individual must not be on parole or violating parole conditions; a current parole violation sanction makes the person ineligible for Food Assistance benefits. It also notes similar ineligibility for current sanctions involving fraudulent representation, sale of firearms, or trafficking Food Assistance valued at $500 or more.

children.title: Fraudulent information
children.summary: States the Food Assistance penalty for fraudulent information: an individual must not be convicted of making a fraudulent representation or statement. If there is a current fraudulent information sanction, the person is ineligible for Food Assistance benefits. This section appears alongside related disqualifications for parole violations, sale of firearms, and trafficking Food Assistance.

children.title: Sale of firearms
children.summary: Individuals are ineligible for Food Assistance if they have a current sanction related to sale of firearms. This section states that a person must not have been found guilty of selling firearms; if they do, the penalty is ineligibility for Food Assistance benefits.

children.title: Trafficking Food Assistance
children.summary: Lists Food Assistance disqualifications tied to specific sanctions. An individual is ineligible if they have a current parole violation sanction, a fraudulent information conviction/sanction, a sale of firearms sanction, or are found guilty of trafficking Food Assistance worth $500 or more. Pages 45–46 are Merative legal notices, including usage permissions, warranty disclaimers, privacy policy, and trademarks.

Overview of Food Assistance sanction assessment. Explains that sanctions are manually created during reassessment when eligible members fail program rules, and penalties depend on prior sanctions and sanction level. Covers violations and penalties for controlled substance sales (2 years, then permanent), IPV (12/24 months, then permanent), work noncompliance and voluntary quit (1/3/6 months escalating), plus ineligibility rules for child support enforcement, drug-related felony, fleeing felony, parole violation, fraudulent information, sale of firearms, and trafficking Food Assistance. Includes only legal notices on pages 45–46.

Overview of Food Assistance sanction assessment. Explains that sanctions are manually created during reassessment when eligible members fail program rules, and caseworkers may sanction or override recommendations. Covers penalty escalation based on prior sanctions for controlled substance sales, IPV, work noncompliance, and voluntary quit, plus fixed ineligibility rules for child support enforcement, drug-related felony, fleeing felon, parole violation, fraudulent information, firearms sales, and Food Assistance trafficking. Pages 45–46 are legal notices only.

Section 1 is the main Food Assistance program guide. It explains eligibility determination for low/no-income households using evidence and business rules, aimed at business analysts rather than technical users. Topics include non-financial requirements (citizenship, alienage, SSN, residency), assistance unit and head-of-household rules, sponsor deeming and battered-alien exceptions, financial calculations (resources, income, deductions, allotment formulas), and sanctions for program violations. Pages 45–46 are legal notices only.
